pages.full_text:
On to Tréguier, very pleasant town on a hill side. A cloister at the Cathedral St. Tugdal. We bought Far Breton in the cake shop in honours of Fatana who gave me the recipe many years ago. After walking around we parked down below the town on the waterside, it's high tide in a tidal estuary. We have a cup of tea.

     We go out for another walk, this time along the Estuary, crossing back + forth across 2 bridges. Then we go into the town, trying to trace the course of the old railway, but it's guesswork. Down by the modern harbour we look in a second hand bookshop + buy a book on coach travel for Eve + Daumier's medical people for John

     Back at the van we had a delicious suppes. Paimpol cider, cheese, pâté, Far Breton + Westfield Rd raspberries,
pages.notes: Page appears to be a continuation from previous spread. 'Fatana' may be a person's name or a place — transcribed as written. 'Westfield Rd raspberries' likely refers to home-grown raspberries from Westfield Road. 'suppes' appears to be written as such (possibly 'supper' abbreviated or a spelling variant). The right edge of the left image shows partial text belonging to the right page.

pages.full_text:
All the vans parked with us along the Estuary (24) seem to be built to prevent enjoyment of the fabulous view up + down the river. The backs are all blocked in + people swivel their front chairs to face backwards while they eat. So only we could enjoy the view.
__Saturday__
We watched the tide go out in the evening. The river was full again at breakfast time. Still no-one else enjoying the glorious view. We walked [?looking at rocks as to—]
We drove north + soon came to [?Plounhinach] [?Clo—]
We wanted to walk along the Sentier des Douaniers but parking was difficult in the first bay we came to. Then we found a less touristy bay + hoped our parking space was allowed.
     Simon had a huge bowl of moules marinières I had a galettes with lots of things in it (buckwheat thin pancake.) Then we had good coffee.
     The weather is warm enough if I wear a sweater + coat, quite a bit of sunshine. The rocks in this poking-up bit of Brittany are amazing, very like Mahabalipuram. Pink granite worn away in parts. Balancing.
pages.notes: The place name after 'came to' is partially obscured/unclear — best reading is 'Plounhinach' but could be 'Plouhinec' or similar Breton place name; the text after it (starting 'Clo—') is cut off at the gutter. 'Mahabalipuram' is the famous rock site in Tamil Nadu, India — used here as a comparison to Brittany granite. The number '(24)' after 'Estuary' may be a count of vans. 'galettes' is a Breton buckwheat pancake, correctly spelled. Simon appears to be a travelling companion. No year is directly stated on this page; context (Brittany trip, volume B covering 2003-2019) does not allow a specific year to be pinned down from this spread alone.
